sections.heading: Overview
sections.content:
Cortex exposes its index through a set of MCP tools. Agents connect via stdio transport and call these tools to build, query, and document a project's code index. All tools take `project_root` as their first argument and return plain text. Errors are returned as `ERROR: {message}` â€” tools never raise exceptions to the caller.

The tools fall into five groups: **index management**, **querying**, **staleness tracking**, **documentation**, and **dFlow workflows**.

sections.heading: Tool Output Standards
sections.content:
All cortex tools follow these output conventions.  When a tool deviates, it is a bug.

**1. Every list operation has a count header**
Any tool that can return more than one result emits a header line before the data:
```
[20 of 87 results â€” fts ranked]
[5 of 5 nodes â€” level 1]
[12 of 34 undocumented nodes]
```
This lets an agent see the total without counting lines.

**2. No silent truncation â€” capped results say so**
If `max_results` cuts the list short, the header says exactly what to do:
```
[20 of 87 results]  (cap=20; pass offset=20 for next page)
```
Tools that return the full set still show the count so the agent knows it is complete.

**3. Every collection-returning tool has `max_results` and `offset`**
`cortex_list`, `cortex_render` (no node_id), and `cortex_list_undocumented` all default to `max_results=60`. Pass a higher value explicitly for full-inventory audits. Use `offset` to page: `offset=60` returns rows 61â€“120.

**4. Summary-first, detail on request**
Tools with potentially large output default to the smallest useful form. `cortex_check` emits one summary line + problems only by default; pass `problems_only=False` for everything. `cortex_render` at level 1 (ID + summary) is the default orientation form â€” use level 2 only when you need the full docstring.

**5. Function-level nodes always include location**
Every tool that renders a node list or graph appends `@ path/file.py#L10-L45` for function-level nodes (those with a line range in their location field). This applies to: `cortex_list`, `cortex_render`, `cortex_search`, `cortex_graph` (all nodes in the tree), and `cortex_render_doc` (linked-nodes section).

**6. Filters are substring-based for human-facing params**
Parameters a human or agent will type â€” `location`, query strings â€” use substring or FTS matching. Exact match is only required for programmatic IDs like `node_id`. `node_type` accepts friendly aliases `"function"` and `"module"` in addition to the internal names.

sections.heading: Index Management
sections.content:
`cortex_build(project_root)` â€” Adds newly-discovered nodes/edges to the index. This is **always discovery-only**: existing nodes are never touched, so `DESC_STALE` / `STRUCTURAL_DRIFT` signals are always preserved. Edges are updated in all cases.

A full rebuild (which resets `code_hash` baselines and clears staleness) is intentionally **not available through this tool**. Use the CLI `cortex build --full` for that, after you have resolved and verified all stale nodes.

Returns: `nodes added`, `nodes unchanged`, `edges updated`, `edges unchanged`, and any ontology warnings.

sections.heading: Querying: Render
sections.content:
`cortex_render(project_root, level, node_id=None, max_results=60, offset=0)` â€” Renders one or all nodes at a chosen detail level.

- Level 0: IDs only â€” use for a quick inventory
- Level 1: ID + one-line summary + file location for function nodes (`@ path/file.py#L10-L45`) â€” the default for orientation
- Level 2: full detail including level_2 documentation body and location
- Level 3: step markers only (dFlow-annotated workflows)

Pass a `node_id` to render a single node (no cap).  Omit `node_id` to render all nodes; output starts with `[N of M nodes â€” level N]` and is capped at `max_results` (default 60). Use `offset` to page.

sections.heading: Querying: List and Graph
sections.content:
`cortex_list(project_root, node_type, tag, parent_id, location, max_results=60, offset=0)` â€” Lists nodes with optional filters.  Pass `location` (file path substring) to list all nodes in a file in one call â€” e.g. `location="snakemake_gen.py"`.  Pass `parent_id` to enumerate children via one-hop `composes` edges â€” works for both module nodes (returns their functions) and doc file nodes (returns their section nodes).  `node_type` accepts `"function"` and `"module"` aliases.  Output: `[N of M results]` header, then one line per node with `@ path#L10-L45` for function nodes.  Use `offset` to page.

`cortex_graph(project_root, node_id, direction, depth)` â€” Traverses edges from a node. `direction` is `"out"`, `"in"`, or `"both"`. All nodes in the output tree include `@ path#L10-L45` for function-level nodes, so agents can jump straight to `read_file` without a follow-up lookup.

sections.heading: Querying: Search
sections.content:
`cortex_search(project_root, query, level=None, max_results=20, node_type=None)` â€” Full-text search over node `level_1` and `level_2` fields. Returns one line per match with a `[N of M results â€” mode]` count header. Each function-node line includes `@ path#L10-L45` so agents can jump straight to `read_file` without a follow-up lookup.

**Query stages** (stops at first stage that returns results):
1. **FTS ranked** â€” BM25-ranked SQLite FTS5. Single word, quoted phrase (`"scan module"`), prefix (`scan*`), or ALL-words-required multi-word. Most queries stop here.
2. **LIKE-AND fallback** â€” substring scan; all tokens must appear somewhere in the text. Fires when FTS returns nothing.
3. **LIKE-OR fallback** â€” substring scan; any token matches. Broad and low-confidence; always capped at 10 regardless of `max_results`. The header says `LIKE-OR fallback (broad, low-confidence)` when this fires â€” usually means the query is too generic; narrow to a single specific term.

The header labels the mode so agents know whether results are ranked or broad:
```
[20 of 87 results â€” fts ranked]
[5 of 5 results â€” LIKE-AND fallback]
[10 of 10 results â€” LIKE-OR fallback (broad, low-confidence)]
```

**Parameters:**
- `level=1` â€” search `level_1` (one-line summaries) only; use when you know the symbol name
- `level=2` â€” search `level_2` (full docstrings) only; use when looking for described behaviour
- `level=None` (default) â€” search both fields
- `max_results` â€” cap on returned rows (default 20); LIKE-OR is always capped at 10. Note: `cortex_search` has no `offset` parameter â€” if you need results beyond the cap, narrow the query or raise `max_results`.
- `node_type` â€” restrict to `"atomic_process"` (functions) or `"composite_process"` (modules); aliases `"function"` and `"module"` are **not** supported here, use the full type names.

sections.heading: Querying: Source
sections.content:
`cortex_source(project_root, node_id)` â€” Returns the raw source body of a node, looked up by ID.

Uses the `level_3_location` stored in the index to locate the file and line range, then returns those source lines directly.  For function-level nodes this is a precise excerpt (`#L10-L45`); for module-level nodes the entire file is returned.

This removes the need to manually parse file paths and line offsets after a search.  The full cortex-only read flow is:

```
cortex_search(project_root, "SymbolName")  â†’  node_id
cortex_source(project_root, node_id)        â†’  source body
```

vs. the previous flow that required a `Read(file, offset, limit)` call.

**Output format:**
```
# myproject::pm.cli::run_pipeline  @ pm/cli.py#L42-L87

<source lines>
```

If the node has no recorded location an `ERROR:` string is returned, consistent with all other cortex tools.

sections.heading: Recommended Agent Patterns
sections.content:
**What cortex is good for (and what it is not)**

Cortex is a staleness and orientation tool, not a call-graph tool. The scanner emits `depends_on` edges from imports and `composes` edges from module â†’ function definitions. It does **not** parse function call sites â€” so `cortex_graph(direction="in")` will not find every place a function is *called*. Use `grep_search` for call-site and usage queries.

What cortex is reliable for:
- Staleness: `cortex_check` instantly confirms which nodes are CLEAN, DESC_STALE, or STRUCTURAL_DRIFT â€” including flagging deprecated/archived subtrees
- Orientation: what modules exist, what is deprecated, what has drifted
- Import dependency: what imports what (`depends_on` edges)
- Documentation coverage: what code has no linked doc sections
- Workflow semantics: `cortex_workflow_list` and `cortex_workflow_detail` expose the purpose, inputs, outputs, step ordering, and coverage links that developers write in dFlow decorators

**Recommended pattern for a cross-cutting change**

1. `cortex_check(project_root)` â€” summary + problems only by default; confirms nothing is already broken before you start
2. `cortex_search(project_root, "SymbolName")` â€” locate the node; result lines include `@ path#L10-L45` for function nodes
3. `cortex_source(project_root, node_id)` â€” read the source body directly; no file path parsing or offset arithmetic needed
4. `grep_search` â€” find call sites and construction points not captured as index edges
5. Make the change, then run `cortex build --full` from the CLI to checkpoint the new baseline (full rebuild, safe because you've already verified staleness)

For day-to-day additions of new files or functions, `cortex_build()` is sufficient â€” it only indexes new nodes and never touches existing baselines.

**Understanding workflow context**

When working on code that is part of a dFlow workflow, use the workflow tools to understand the semantic context:

1. `cortex_workflow_list(module="staleness")` â€” find all workflows touching this file area
2. `cortex_workflow_detail(workflow_id="run_pipeline")` â€” see step ordering and what each step delegates to
3. Use the cortex node IDs from the output with `cortex_source` or `cortex_render` to inspect implementations

The workflow tools are the semantic entry point (purpose, inputs, outputs); cortex tools are the structural entry point (code, dependencies, staleness).

**Context cost**

`cortex_check` now defaults to `problems_only=True`, emitting a one-line summary and only non-CLEAN rows. On a clean project this is two lines total. `cortex_search` and `cortex_list` results include `@ path#L10-L45` for function nodes. `cortex_source` completes the loop: once you have a node ID, the source body is one more call â€” no file path parsing or offset arithmetic needed.

**Listing all functions in a file (one call)**

Instead of searching for a node ID and then calling `cortex_list(parent_id=...)`, use `cortex_list(location="file.py")` directly. The `location` parameter is a case-sensitive substring match on `level_3_location`, so partial names work. Add `node_type="function"` to restrict to functions only.

sections.heading: Staleness: Check and History
sections.content:
`cortex_check(project_root, problems_only=True)` â€” Reports staleness status for every node: `CLEAN`, `DESC_STALE`, `STRUCTURAL_DRIFT`, or `UNVERIFIED`.

Always emits a one-line summary first:
```
3 DESC_STALE Â· 1 STRUCTURAL_DRIFT Â· 0 UNVERIFIED Â· 241 CLEAN
```

With `problems_only=True` (default) only non-CLEAN rows follow â€” output is small enough to read directly. When all nodes are clean the output is just the summary line and `(all nodes CLEAN)`. Pass `problems_only=False` for a full inventory audit.

Also appends a DOC SECTIONS block showing stale linked documentation sections (problems_only=True shows only stale sections; False shows all).

`cortex_history(project_root, node_id, limit)` â€” Shows the change history for a single node newest-first. Annotates the open stale window (code changed, description not updated) with the number of days it has been open.

sections.heading: Staleness: Mark Clean
sections.content: `cortex_mark_clean(project_root, node_id, reason)` â€” Records an `AGENT_VERIFIED` history row on a `DESC_STALE` node. Use only after reading both the current code and documentation and confirming they are consistent. The reason string is stored and surfaced in `cortex_history` for human review before push.

sections.heading: Documentation: Write and Link
sections.content:
`cortex_write_doc(project_root, doc_json)` â€” Accepts a JSON string describing a documentation document, writes it to `docs/{id}.json`, and immediately indexes it. Validates that all linked `node_id` values exist; unknown IDs are returned as warnings (the write still succeeds).

`cortex_add_link(project_root, section_id, node_id)` â€” Appends a link from an existing doc section to a code node. Loads the JSON file, adds the link, writes it back, and re-indexes. Section IDs follow the pattern `{project_id}::docs.{doc_id}::{section_id}`.

`cortex_list_undocumented(project_root, node_type, max_results=60, offset=0)` â€” Lists all nodes with no inbound `documents` edge. Use this to find coverage gaps. Output starts with a `[N of M undocumented nodes]` header. Accepts `"function"` and `"module"` as `node_type` aliases.

sections.heading: Documentation: Render
sections.content: `cortex_render_doc(project_root, doc_id)` â€” Renders a DocJSON document as Markdown, with linked node summaries listed under each section. Each section heading is annotated with its section ID (e.g. `<!-- id: my-section-id -->`), so agents can call `cortex_update_section` immediately after reading without a separate enumeration step. Each linked function node includes a `@ path#L10-L45` suffix. Pass `doc_id="list"` to see all indexed document IDs.

sections.heading: dFlow Workflows
sections.content:
Two tools provide agent access to dFlow workflow metadata stored in `.dflow/workflow.db`. These tools are the **semantic entry point** â€” they expose the `purpose=`, `inputs=`, `outputs=`, and step ordering that developers write in dFlow decorators. Cortex tools (`cortex_render`, `cortex_graph`, `cortex_source`) remain the **structural entry point** for implementation detail. The workflow tools include cortex node IDs in their output so agents can cross from one world to the other.

Both tools return graceful messages when no dFlow database is present.

`cortex_workflow_list(project_root, module=None, role=None, max_results=30, offset=0)` â€” Lists all `@workflow`, `@task`, and `@test_workflow` functions. Returns one line per function with: name, decorator role, purpose summary, file location, and the corresponding cortex node ID (when resolved). Filter by `module` (file path substring, e.g. `module="staleness"`) or `role` (`"workflow"`, `"task"`, `"test_workflow"`). Paginated with `max_results` / `offset`.

`cortex_workflow_detail(project_root, workflow_id, verbose=False)` â€” Shows a single workflow's ordered steps. `workflow_id` is the function name or dFlow integer ID from the list output. Default output shows file location, cortex node ID, and ordered steps with delegate targets (cortex node IDs). Pass `verbose=True` to also show `purpose`, `inputs`, `outputs`, and `critical` fields on both the workflow header and each step. For `@test_workflow` functions, also lists coverage targets (`validates` links).

**Recommended agent flow:**
1. `cortex_workflow_list(module="pipeline")` â†’ discover workflows in a module area
2. `cortex_workflow_detail(workflow_id="run_pipeline")` â†’ see ordered steps and delegates
3. `cortex_render(node_id=...)` or `cortex_source(node_id=...)` â†’ inspect implementation of a specific step target

**Future (ADR-005 / ADR-009):** When dFlow and Cortex are unified into a single database (ADR-005), these tools will switch their backend queries but keep the same interface. When unified staleness (ADR-009) lands, workflow list output will include staleness badges and `cortex_workflow_detail` will show per-step staleness.
